Company Overview: PSYONIC builds advanced bionic limbs — most notably the Ability Hand, a prosthetic with 32 grip patterns and touch feedback — for upper-limb amputees. Their devices must perform reliably on human bodies under real-world stress, where a control software failure or mechanical decision made wrong isn't a UX problem, it's a patient safety event. As they scale from startup to commercial medical device company, every hardware-software integration decision carries both clinical and regulatory weight.
ICP Fit: Strong fit — medical device hardware company making irreversible design decisions under FDA scrutiny, where a wrong call on control systems or sensor integration creates patient harm, recall risk, and reputational damage that compounds over time.
Signal Detail: PSYONIC just demoed at the 2025 Robotics Summit and San Diego Comic-Con, signaling an active commercial push beyond early adopters. Their principal engineer's LinkedIn shows active development of the Ability Hand neuromuscular control interface with third-party pattern recognition integrations (Coapt). Expanding team plus new hardware-software integration points at scale is exactly when architecture and validation decisions get made fast and get locked in — often before the downstream consequences are visible.
Next Action: LinkedIn DM to Aadeel Akhtar (http://www.linkedin.com/in/aadeelakhtar)
CEO Notes: Founder-CEO with a neuroscience and engineering PhD who cares deeply about patient outcomes — lead with clinical risk and irreversible design decisions, not efficiency or speed. If you meet him at a robotics or med-tech event, ask about the control software integration architecture and how they're thinking about validation as third-party device partnerships grow. High-conviction fit; small but technically serious team making real hardware decisions under FDA pressure.
First Touch Message: Aadeel — noticed PSYONIC is actively expanding the Ability Hand's control stack, including third-party integrations like Coapt. As you scale those integration surfaces, the decisions made now about validation and failure-mode handling tend to get baked in permanently. Engineering teams at this stage often find out which calls were wrong only after they're expensive to reverse. Worth a 20-minute conversation about where those pressure points usually emerge?

ICP Fit: Strong — IonQ is scaling from $130M to $235M revenue in a single year, making irreversible architecture and infrastructure decisions at speed, with reputational and financial consequences if quantum system reliability or engineering execution falls short in front of marquee enterprise and government customers.

Engineering decisions around qubit fidelity, error correction, and hardware-software integration determine whether customer workloads actually run — failures are visible, public, and expensive.
ICP Fit: Strong — IonQ is scaling from $130M to $235M revenue in a single year, making irreversible architecture and infrastructure decisions at speed, with reputational and financial consequences if quantum system reliability or engineering execution falls short in front of marquee enterprise and government customers.
Signal Detail: IonQ just reported 202% YoY revenue growth ($130M in 2025) and is guiding $235M for 2026 — the first publicly traded quantum company to cross $100M. They're simultaneously hiring across 72 engineering roles, expanding into full-stack quantum, and integrating recent acquisitions (Skyloom Global). That combination — rapid revenue scaling, M&A integration, and aggressive hiring — creates exactly the kind of high-stakes technical decision pressure where teams move fast and regret decisions made without sufficient rigor.

Company Overview: Neurable builds EEG sensors embedded in consumer headphones that track brain activity — focus, cognitive load, reaction speed — in real time. They serve gaming, workplace productivity, and human behavior research markets, with partnerships including HyperX and iMotions. Engineering decisions around signal fidelity, sensor miniaturization, and data interpretation directly affect product credibility and user trust in a field where bad data or overstated claims can collapse commercial relationships.
ICP Fit: Strong — Neurable makes irreversible hardware-software architecture decisions (sensor design, EEG signal processing pipelines, BCI algorithms) where being wrong means flawed neuroscience products at scale, reputational collapse in a credibility-sensitive field, and lost enterprise partnerships that don't come back.
Signal Detail: CES 2026 demo of their EEG headset generated public scrutiny on real-time brain signal accuracy, and their October 2025 iMotions integration puts their data quality under academic and enterprise research review — a context where signal noise or misinterpretation carries institutional credibility risk. They're also actively hiring neurotechnology engineers, signaling they're scaling technical decision-making fast, which is exactly when architecture and validation choices become hard to reverse.
Next Action: LinkedIn DM to Ramses Alcaide (http://www.linkedin.com/in/pharoramses)
CEO Notes: High-priority founder-to-founder conversation — Ramses built Neurable from his PhD research and has deep personal stakes in scientific credibility. At an event, lead with the tension between consumer-grade EEG and research-grade validation, not with product features. He'll care about whether his pipeline can survive institutional scrutiny as the iMotions partnership pulls him into harder use cases.
First Touch Message: Ramses — saw the CES coverage and the iMotions integration. When EEG data moves into research platforms, the bar for signal validity shifts from 'good enough for consumer UX' to 'defensible under peer review.' That's a different kind of pressure on your processing pipeline. Curious whether the team has stress-tested that gap as you scale into enterprise and academic channels. Worth a conversation?

Company Overview: 0xbow builds Privacy Pools, a compliant on-chain privacy protocol for Ethereum that lets users prove fund legitimacy without revealing transaction history. They serve crypto users and DeFi protocols that need privacy without regulatory exposure. Every architectural decision — from proof generation to association set design — directly determines whether the protocol survives regulatory scrutiny or gets delisted.
ICP Fit: Strong — post-seed crypto infrastructure company where a single wrong technical decision (flawed ZK circuit, broken compliance logic, or insecure withdrawal flow) creates irreversible reputational, legal, and financial damage at protocol scale.
Signal Detail: 0xbow just closed a $3.5M seed round in November 2025 and announced plans to hire engineers and expand to additional blockchains. The Ethereum Foundation integration validates the protocol, but multi-chain expansion means rebuilding and stress-testing core privacy and compliance assumptions across new execution environments — exactly the moment teams overextend and introduce architectural debt they can't unwind.
Next Action: LinkedIn DM to Ameen Soleimani (http://www.linkedin.com/in/ameen-soleimani-97181942)
CEO Notes: High-value account — freshly funded, technically ambitious, and operating in a space where one bad decision is a protocol obituary. If you meet Ameen at an event, lead with the multi-chain expansion risk angle: compliance logic that works on Ethereum is not portable by default, and they're about to find that out. He's a builder who respects technical specificity — skip the sales talk, go straight to the hard problem.
First Touch Message: Ameen — saw the $3.5M close and the multi-chain expansion plans. Privacy Pools works on Ethereum because every compliance assumption was tuned to that environment. Porting the protocol means re-validating those assumptions chain by chain — proof systems, finality guarantees, association set integrity. Teams that move fast on that usually find the gaps after launch, not before. Worth a conversation about where those decisions get hardest?

Company Overview: Adan (Association pour le Développement des Actifs Numériques) is the leading French crypto and digital assets industry association, representing companies building in the Web3 and crypto space across Europe. Their decisions on regulatory positioning and technical standards directly affect the compliance and product roadmaps of dozens of member companies.
ICP Fit: Moderate — Adan itself is a trade association, not an engineering team, but they sit at the center of high-stakes technical and regulatory decisions that affect their entire membership, making them a strong multiplier contact if the angle is right.
Signal Detail: Claire Balva joined as CEO in September 2025 — a clear mandate to elevate Adan's influence and output. New leadership typically audits how the organization supports members through complex decisions. With MiCA implementation accelerating across the EU and member firms facing compliance architecture choices right now, Adan is under pressure to provide sharper technical and strategic guidance, not just policy commentary.

Company Overview: Aktionariat builds tokenization infrastructure that lets Swiss private companies issue and trade blockchain-based shares directly on their own websites. They serve 70+ Swiss SMEs and 30,000+ users who rely on smart contracts for legally binding equity transactions. Engineering mistakes here aren't bugs — they're securities fraud or irreversible cap table damage.
ICP Fit: Strong — tokenized equity infrastructure carries all five signals: legal/financial/reputational cost of being wrong is severe, errors in smart contract logic or share register state are persistent and hard to unwind, they're clearly aware of the risk (they've built compliance tooling), decisions compound as they scale issuers, and a bad technical call on chain selection or secondary market mechanics could permanently strand 30,000 users.
Signal Detail: Two live signals converge right now: (1) Aktionariat just launched 'Secondaries,' their first blockchain-based secondary trading tool — a meaningful architectural leap that introduces new attack surface, liquidity logic, and regulatory exposure they haven't had to defend before. (2) The Taurus partnership extends their infrastructure into a regulated custodian's stack, meaning their technical decisions now affect a third party's compliance posture. Both expansions happened in 2024-2025, suggesting the engineering team is making high-stakes architectural calls under commercial pressure.
Next Action: LinkedIn DM to Murat Ogat (http://www.linkedin.com/in/muratogat)
CEO Notes: High priority — Murat is both Co-Founder and CEO (also previously CTO), so he holds technical and commercial context in one person. If you meet at EthCC or a tokenization event, lead with the Secondaries launch and ask how they're thinking about decision governance as they move from issuance infrastructure into active trading. He'll engage on the engineering risk angle because he built the stack himself.
First Touch Message: Murat — noticed Aktionariat launched Secondaries and closed the Taurus partnership within a short window. Secondary trading mechanics and custodian integrations are the two places where assumptions that worked in a closed issuance loop tend to break under real trading conditions. Curious whether the team is stress-testing the decision framework the same way they stress-test the contracts — or whether that's a gap worth a quick conversation.

Company Overview: Belem Capital is a Luxembourg-based digital asset investment vehicle that recently absorbed MEV Capital's institutional asset management team following an 80% AUM collapse over four months. They manage exposure to DeFi strategies and onchain yield products for institutional investors. Their decisions carry high stakes because capital allocation errors in volatile crypto markets are both immediate and difficult to reverse.
ICP Fit: Moderate — they operate in a high-stakes financial environment where execution errors are costly and persistent, but the recent restructuring (team contraction from 15 to 5, revenue down 86.8% in one quarter) suggests they may be in survival mode rather than investing in new tooling.
Signal Detail: Belem just internalized MEV Capital's team mid-crisis — absorbing new risk and execution frameworks while AUM dropped from ~$1.5B to roughly $300M in four months. That integration under pressure is exactly when decision-making infrastructure breaks down. They're consolidating without a proven internal process, which creates real exposure.
Next Action: LinkedIn DM to Gonzague Tournelle (http://www.linkedin.com/in/gonzaguedelatournelle)
CEO Notes: Interesting but fragile account — they're mid-restructuring after a major AUM collapse. The angle is operational resilience and decision quality under pressure, not growth. Worth a light touch if you cross paths at a crypto or institutional DeFi event, but don't invest heavily until they stabilize.
First Touch Message: Gonzague — noticed Belem absorbed MEV Capital's team as AUM was contracting sharply. Integrating a new risk and execution framework mid-drawdown, with a smaller team, is one of the harder moments to get decisions right — the margin for error shrinks just as the complexity grows. Worth a short conversation about how teams in that position typically approach high-stakes calls under pressure?

Company Overview: Babylon Software (babylon.com) builds translation, dictionary, and information-lookup tools used by millions of users and enterprise customers globally. Their core product — instant single-click text translation and search — sits at the intersection of language technology and productivity software. Engineering decisions around language models, data pipelines, and platform integrations affect a large installed user base, making reliability and accuracy failures highly visible.
ICP Fit: Moderate — established software company with a real engineering team making product and infrastructure decisions, though the unclear current scale and ambiguous product roadmap direction make it hard to confirm active high-stakes technical decision cycles without more discovery.
Signal Detail: Babylon Software's 'Bridges by Babylon' product page lists Joe Arnon as CTO, suggesting an active engineering leadership structure. The company appears to be evolving beyond classic desktop translation software toward newer integration layers. No major recent funding or hiring spike is visible, but the presence of both a CTO and CFO (Shanit Tsfoni) in a company of unknown size suggests a leadership team actively balancing build-vs-buy and platform decisions — a natural pressure point for our positioning. Worth investigating whether they're modernizing their core language pipeline or expanding API integrations.
Next Action: LinkedIn DM to Joe Arnon (http://www.linkedin.com/in/joearnon)
CEO Notes: Niche but real — Babylon has genuine engineering leadership and a product in transition, which is exactly when technical decision risk peaks. If you meet Joe, lead with the cost of platform missteps at a company where the core IP is the software itself — wrong architecture choices don't just slow them down, they erode the product advantage directly.
First Touch Message: Joe — noticed Babylon is evolving the core platform with products like Bridges, which usually means the team is navigating some real architectural tradeoffs: what to rebuild, what to extend, and where a wrong call becomes expensive to unwind. Those decisions tend to move faster than the confidence behind them. Would it be worth a short conversation about how other engineering teams in similar transitions have structured those calls?

Company Overview: Block Analitica provides quantitative risk intelligence for DeFi protocols, serving major platforms like Sky (MakerDAO) and Spark, which together secure over $8 billion in funds. Their work involves setting risk parameters, oracle configurations, and collateral caps where a wrong call can trigger cascading liquidations or bad debt at protocol scale. They recently caught and revoked a faulty sDAI/WETH market oracle on Morpho before it caused damage — the kind of high-stakes decision that defines their daily work.
ICP Fit: Strong fit — they make irreversible, high-frequency risk decisions on live protocols where being wrong costs billions in user funds and destroys protocol reputation, they are actively hiring quantitative risk analysts signaling growth, and clarity on marginal risk calls directly accelerates their ability to take on more protocols.
Signal Detail: Block Analitica is actively recruiting quantitative risk analysts for Sky Ecosystem work, signaling they are scaling their risk coverage capacity. Simultaneously, they are taking on more protocol relationships (Euler V2, M0, KEEP Network, Morpho vaults) — more protocols means more edge-case decisions under time pressure with incomplete data. The sDAI/WETH oracle incident they caught and revoked is a public signal that their decision process is already under operational stress at current scale.
Next Action: Send LinkedIn DM to Shatabarto Bhattacharya (http://www.linkedin.com/in/shatabarto-bhattacharya-6bb6ba149)
CEO Notes: High-priority account — Block Analitica sits at the risk decision layer for $8B+ in DeFi collateral and is scaling fast. If you meet Primoz Kordez (Founder) at an event, lead with the operational tension between growing protocol coverage and maintaining decision quality per analyst — that is the existential scaling problem for a firm like his.

Worth a 20-minute conversation?
